meta.name: Healthcare SERVQUAL TRIZ Contradiction Matrix
meta.author: Jiun-Yih Lee, Pei-Shan Lee, Cheng-Hsien Chiang, Yi-Ping Chen, Chiung-Ju Chen, Yuan-Ming Huang, Jlan-Ren Chiu, Pei-Ching Yang, Chen-An Yeh, Jui-Ting Chang
meta.notes: Hybrid matrix with two parameter types: S-prefixed SERVQUAL improving parameters (S1-S10) and T-prefixed classical TRIZ worsening parameters (T19, T25, T9, T36, T26, T27, T29, T28, T34). Matrix keys use these prefixes: matrix[S-param][T-param] = [principle IDs].

S1.name: Access
S1.description: Approachability and ease of contact with the healthcare service.

S2.name: Communication
S2.description: Keeping customers informed in language they can understand and listening to them.

S3.name: Understanding
S3.description: Making the effort to know customers and their needs (understanding the customer).

S4.name: Empathy
S4.description: Caring, individualized attention the firm provides its customers.

S5.name: Reliability
S5.description: Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.

S6.name: Tangibles
S6.description: Physical facilities, equipment, and appearance of personnel.

S7.name: Responsiveness: Time
S7.description: Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service, with emphasis on time aspects.

S8.name: Responsiveness: Speed
S8.description: Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service, with emphasis on speed/throughput aspects.

S9.name: Competence
S9.description: Possession of the required skills and knowledge to perform the service.

S10.name: Assurance
S10.description: Knowledge and courtesy of employees and their ability to inspire trust and confidence.

T19.name: Energy spent by a moving object
T19.description: The measure of the object's capacity for doing work. Classical TRIZ parameter #19 (Use of energy by moving object). In healthcare context: energy/effort expenditure during service delivery processes.

T25.name: Loss of time
T25.description: Time is the duration of an activity. Improving the loss of time means reducing the time taken for the activity. Classical TRIZ parameter #25. In healthcare context: time lost during service processes such as reporting or administrative tasks.

T9.name: Speed
T9.description: The velocity of an object; the rate of a process or action in time. Classical TRIZ parameter #9. In healthcare context: rate at which service processes are completed.

T36.name: Complexity of a device
T36.description: The number and diversity of elements and element interrelationships within a system. Classical TRIZ parameter #36 (Device complexity). In healthcare context: complexity of service delivery systems and processes.

T26.name: Amount of substance
T26.description: The number or amount of a system's materials, substances, parts or subsystems. Classical TRIZ parameter #26 (Quantity of substance/the matter). In healthcare context: physical resources, materials, and supplies required.

T27.name: Reliability
T27.description: A system's ability to perform its intended functions in predictable ways and conditions. Classical TRIZ parameter #27. In healthcare context: dependability and consistency of service systems.

T29.name: Accuracy of manufacturing
T29.description: The extent to which the actual characteristics of the system or object match the specified or required characteristics. Classical TRIZ parameter #29 (Manufacturing precision). In healthcare context: precision and correctness of service delivery processes.

T28.name: Accuracy of measurement
T28.description: The closeness of the measured value to the actual value of a property of a system. Classical TRIZ parameter #28 (Measurement accuracy). In healthcare context: accuracy of assessment, monitoring, and reporting systems.

T34.name: Repairability
T34.description: Quality characteristics such as convenience, comfort, simplicity, and time to repair faults, failures, or defects in a system. Classical TRIZ parameter #34 (Ease of repair). In healthcare context: ability to correct and recover from service failures or process defects.

1.name: Segmentation
1.description: Divide an object into independent parts.
sub_principles: a) Divide an object into independent parts.
b) Make an object easy to disassemble.
c) Increase the degree of fragmentation or segmentation.

2.name: Taking out (Extraction)
2.description: Separate an interfering part or property from an object, or single out the only necessary part (or property) of an object.
sub_principles: a) Separate an interfering part or property from an object, or single out the only necessary part (or property) of an object.

4.name: Asymmetry
4.description: Change the shape of an object from symmetrical to asymmetrical.
sub_principles: a) Change the shape of an object from symmetrical to asymmetrical.
b) If an object is asymmetrical, increase its degree of asymmetry.

10.name: Preliminary action (Prior action)
10.description: Perform, before it is needed, the required change of an object (either fully or partially).
sub_principles: a) Perform, before it is needed, the required change of an object (either fully or partially).
b) Pre-arrange objects such that they can come into action from the most convenient place and without losing time for their delivery.

13.name: The other way round (Doing it in reverse)
13.description: Invert the action(s) used to obtain the desired effect.
sub_principles: a) Invert the action(s) used to obtain the desired effect (e.g. instead of cooling an object, heat it).
b) Make movable parts (or the external environment) fixed, and fixed parts movable.
c) Turn the object (or process) upside down.

14.name: Spheroidality (Curvature)
14.description: Instead of using rectilinear parts, surfaces, or forms, use curvilinear ones.
sub_principles: a) Instead of using rectilinear parts, surfaces, or forms, use curvilinear ones; move from flat surfaces to spherical ones; from parts shaped as a cube (parallelepiped) to ball-shaped structures.
b) Use rollers, balls, spirals, domes.
c) Go from linear to rotary motion, use centrifugal forces.

18.name: Mechanical vibration
18.description: Cause an object to oscillate or vibrate.
sub_principles: a) Cause an object to oscillate or vibrate.
b) Increase its frequency (even up to the ultrasonic).
c) Use an object's resonant frequency.
d) Use piezoelectric vibrators instead of mechanical ones.
e) Use combined ultrasonic and electromagnetic field oscillations.

22.name: Blessing in disguise (Conversion of harm into benefit)
22.description: Use harmful factors (particularly, harmful effects of the environment or surroundings) to achieve a positive effect.
sub_principles: a) Use harmful factors (particularly, harmful effects of the environment or surroundings) to achieve a positive effect.
b) Eliminate the primary harmful action by adding it to another harmful action to resolve the problem.
c) Amplify a harmful factor to such a degree that it is no longer harmful.

24.name: Intermediary (Mediator)
24.description: Use an intermediary carrier article or intermediary process.
sub_principles: a) Use an intermediary carrier article or intermediary process.
b) Merge one object temporarily with another (which can be easily removed).

25.name: Self-service
25.description: Make an object serve itself by performing auxiliary helpful functions.
sub_principles: a) Make an object serve itself by performing auxiliary helpful functions.
b) Use waste resources, energy, or substances.

26.name: Copying
26.description: Instead of an unavailable, expensive, fragile object, use simpler and inexpensive copies.
sub_principles: a) Instead of an unavailable, expensive, fragile object, use simpler and inexpensive copies.
b) Replace an object, or process, with optical copies.
c) If visible optical copies are already used, move to infrared or ultraviolet copies.

28.name: Mechanics substitution (Replacement of mechanical system)
28.description: Replace a mechanical means with a sensory (optical, acoustic, taste or smell) means.
sub_principles: a) Replace a mechanical means with a sensory (optical, acoustic, taste or smell) means.
b) Use electric, magnetic and electromagnetic fields to interact with the object.
c) Change from static to movable fields, from unstructured fields to those having structure.
d) Use fields in conjunction with field-activated (e.g. ferromagnetic) particles.

30.name: Flexible shells and thin films
30.description: Use flexible shells and thin films instead of three-dimensional structures.
sub_principles: a) Use flexible shells and thin films instead of three-dimensional structures.
b) Isolate the object from the external environment using flexible shells and thin films.

32.name: Color changes (Change of colour)
32.description: Change the color of an object or its external environment.
sub_principles: a) Change the color of an object or its external environment.
b) Change the transparency of an object or its external environment.

34.name: Discarding and recovering (Rejection and regeneration of parts)
34.description: Make portions of an object that have fulfilled their functions go away or modify them directly during operation.
sub_principles: a) Make portions of an object that have fulfilled their functions go away (discard by dissolving, evaporating, etc.) or modify them directly during operation.
b) Conversely, restore consumable parts of an object directly in operation.

35.name: Parameter changes (Translation of properties)
35.description: Change an object's physical state (e.g. to a gas, liquid, or solid).
sub_principles: a) Change an object's physical state (e.g. to a gas, liquid, or solid).
b) Change the concentration or consistency.
c) Change the degree of flexibility.
d) Change the temperature.

36.name: Phase transitions
36.description: Use phenomena occurring during phase transitions (e.g. volume changes, loss or absorption of heat, etc.).
sub_principles: a) Use phenomena occurring during phase transitions (e.g. volume changes, loss or absorption of heat, etc.).
